sentences.text: George-- George Washington.
In Washington.
My granddaughter goes to Connecticut, Connecticut College.
The younger one is 12, 10, and eight.
Oh yeah.
The older one is going to be barmitzvah next year.
He's-- he's-- a big kid.
He is.
His father is big.
My daughter's like me, small.
My name is Miriam Moskowitz.
My maiden name is Inowroclawska.
I was born in Lodz, Poland.
September 15, 1925.
In Lodz, Poland.
Lodz was a very big city, and it was really a Jewish city.
The only one in our quarter that I could remember that we lived, which was all Jews.
There was only the janitor that was non-Jews, non-Jewish.
The rest of them were all Jews.
The janitor.
Right.
Lodz was predominantly a city of silk, manufacturers of-- of silk.
And it was a lot of business.
And just like my father was working a very big factory.
He was a mechanical engineer that was working in a big, big factory of plywood and home improvements.
Mostly orthodox.
As my early recor-- recollections, we-- my family belonged to an orthodox temple.
And also when it came Sabba-- Shabbat, we could see throngs of Jews with the talleisim under their arm and with a kittel, with the shtreimel.
And it was predominately an orthodox city.
My parents were orthodox.
My home was orthodox.
I was brought up-- my mother was very observant and my father too, and he used to attend temple regularly.
And Shabbat was a very holy day in our house, and every holiday.
And unfortunately I was 13 years old when the war broke out, but till then, I have very fond recollection of my childhood.
My mother was a wonderful homemaker, and Shabbat was a very big feast in our house--
--with friends and families and all the goodies on the table.
And my father used to go early Shabbat to temple and bless all the children, the same thing as of yontiff.
And those are my recollections of childhood.
When I was young, when I was almost five years old, I used to attend Bais Yaakov, and I used to learn how to read and write Hebrew and-- and Yiddish.
And then a rabbi used to come to my house.
When he taught my older brother for the bar mitzvah, he used to also teach me Hebrew and Jewish.
Yes.
During the day, I attended a school.
It was predominately girls.
It was elementary school from-- from the city.
And it was Polish, speaking Polish.
And we used to recite their prayers. and we Jewish girls used to stand with bowed heads and not to recite anything.
But that was our daily routine.
Now going to school, I remember since childhood, we used to go through different roads because we used to be stoned from the Polish children.
So we used to eliminate certain streets, but they found us anyway.
We used to be stoned and screamed at us with all kind of slurs, with all kind of names, with dirty Jews.
When I was a little girl, the only thing that I could remember, all the walls used to be covered-- death to Jews and don't buy by Jewish stores, or Jews go to Palestine.
Those were my early recollection since childhood.
Well, the only thing that I could remember that I was hit many times with stones before entering the school.
Yes.
Yes.
No, not in Poland, not before the war.
I had three brothers.
My older brother was two years older than I was.
And I was the second child.
Then I had my younger brother.
And then just before the war in 1938, my youngest brother arrived.
And when they took us away, he was over a year old.
My older brother was Kasria Tuvia.
My younger brother was Moshe, and my youngest was Godel.
My father's name was Schmuel Israel.
My mother's name was Hanna.
My mother's maiden name was Gassman.
My father's name was Inowroclawski.
When the war broke out, I just finished my seventh grade of public school.
I beg your pardon?
My life was affected because my parents, just like any other parents today, have high dreams, high hopes, for their children.
And I guess my parents had high hopes for me, too.
And my schooling days were interrupted, and on the Hanukkah day, 1939, the second day of Hanukkah, the candles were on the table, and after midnight, our front door broke-- was broken in.
And the Germans broke into my house, and they gave us 10 minutes to leave.
We gathered whatever it was possible in 10 minutes.
My mother grabbed the baby, and we're whatever-- whatever to take.
And we were loaded up into trucks downstairs.
And we were taken to [?
Radogoszcz. ?] This was a big factory in Lodz, and we were gathered together with it-- in trucks.
When we arrived over there, the place was already full with Jews, with children.
And when we arrived, we had nothing to eat and nothing to drink.
And early in the morning, we were gathered and lined up and loaded again into trucks, and we were taken to the trains.
Well, it was always very big anti-Semitism.
We were always beaten or-- or taunted as Jews.
And when the war broke out, it was terrible.
When they mar-- when the Germans marched into Lodz, we couldn't visualize how bad it would be.
Nobody did.
We were right away suffering hunger because nobody worked.
We were restricted to-- to our own homes, and we had to stay in lines all night for a piece of bread.
My father and I and my older brother, we used to stay all night in the line for a piece of bread.
But unfortunately, after staying a whole night in the line, towards morning, the Germans just arrived and pick out all the Jews-- whoever looked Jew, anything as Jews.
They were picked up and beaten up, which my father unfortunately was beaten up many times.
And one of the evenings, one of the nights that I stayed in the line, I witnessed the burning of-- of my temple, my shul, that we used to attend all our lifes.
Our [NON-ENGLISH] shul was in flames.
And my father, towards morning, when he was picked out from the line, not getting bread, he went over to the German because my father was very fluent in German language.
He finished his university, his schooling-- he finished in Germany before the war in Berlin, and he got his degree as a mechanical engineer from the University of Berlin.
So he had experience in the First World War with his classmates, Germans, as well educated people, and he couldn't imagine the torture or the inhuman treatment toward another human being.
So he tried to get another piece of bread, going over to the German and begging that he is a former student of Germany.
And speaking in German, it didn't help.
He was beaten up and throw in the gutter.
And we, my brother and I, we brought him home, all torn up, his clothes, and beaten up.
And we used to beg our father not to do it again, but the hunger was more than anything.
We were four children in the house.
And he tried many times, and unfortunately, he was beaten up many times.
And during the day, it was a very big hazard to walk out in the street because they used to catch Jews in the street, and they used to load them up into truck, and they vanished.
We never saw these people.
Or they used to catch rabbis, and they used to mock them in the middle of the street.
They used to make a show out of it.
They used to cut half of the beard and half of the payos.
And he used to say, you are a believer of God.
Where is your God now?
Show me.
And then they opened a buckle that they used to have on their uniform, and they used to say, you see?
This is our God.
We believe in God, and God is with uns, not with you.
And they used to torture every Jew that they caught.
And they used to take pictures out of fun.
And you see--
Yes.
I was the only girl in the house, and I always used to wear to disguise myself because we used to wear a Jewish star on our hand.
And I used to take off my Jewish star, and I used to put on a babushka on my hair to look like a non-Jew.
And I used to walk the street, which I was risking it, but I went-- I was able, this way, to go into a store and to get something to eat for the family.
And I witnessed a lot of scenes like that.
In 1939 when the war broke out.
As soon they-- and as soon they marched in into our town, my-- my city unfortunately became their-- not Lodz.
It became Litzmannstadt.
Litzmannstadt became one of their city.
It belonged to them, like a German city.
And they occupied it, and they treated it-- anybody, any Jew was their trespasser, and they treated us likewise with all the cruel methods.
You see, we had no way of leaving.
A lot of people that they were well off, through certain channels, they were able to leave.
Unfortunately, we were six people in our house, and my father was always working.
Considering we weren't poor, but we weren't rich either.
Well, our house never lacked of anything.
We had everything in our house.
We had everything.
But when it came out the war when my father stopped working, we were without funds, and I guess we were unable to leave at the time.
And nobody visualized that it's going to be that bad that we have to leave.
It formed a Judenrate.
It formed a Judenrate, which they also were talking that-- that in due time, they're going to make a ghetto.
And they also advised, the Judenrate advised, that whoever has the means of leaving-- that means if you have enough money-- that you could maybe salvage your life.
But at the time, one of my mother's brother came up to our house suggesting to my mother that he's going away to Bialystok, and he would like that my older brother should go with him.
And in due time, maybe he could send somebody to bring our whole family and his, because he had a wife and two children too.
But my mother was against it for my older brother to leave because she felt as long we are together as a family, maybe we have a chance of surviving.
But unfortunately proved no good because after the war, my uncle did survive in Russia.
He was pushed-- from Bialystok, he went into Russia and survived.
But my older brother, I never saw him after the war.
He never survived.
Prominent Jews from the city.
No.
You see, as the war broke out, we lived in a court-- as I said, they were all orthodox Jews-- and in our court were five steibelach.
And as soon the war broke out, we were afraid that they will burn just like they burned the synagogues.
So the heads of the steibelach distributed all the Sefer torahs to all the Jewish people that lived in the court.
And we also had a Sefer Torah in our house.
And in fact, just before we were taken from our home, my father took one of the Sefer Torahs and put in a velvet bedspread and covered it.

And took me and my older brother-- we had a coal bin in the hallway-- and he ripped a part of the floor and dug deep enough to hold the Sefer Torah and put in the Sefer Torah and my mother's jewelry and all the silver from the house, the candlesticks and so forth, whatever was in the house and brought me and my older brother into the coal bin.
And my father and mother said to us both, there is a war going on, and we don't know what's going to happen.
But remember, whoever will survive, if we survive, you should remember that these things are here for you to uncover, and covered back with you with the boards and covered back with the coal.

And this way-- but unfortunately, after the war, it was ripped apart because when I was liberated, not just the floor was ripped, but the walls were torn apart and nothing was there when I came to look for it.
Yes.
We were young children.
We couldn't imagine that something will-- so treacherous will become of all the situation.
We thought maybe we're going to be resettled someplace in a different city or whatever, but we didn't know what the future is holding for us.
Mostly we took certain pictures.
Mostly we took certain things of clothing or bedding, the littlest possible we could get in 10 minutes to get out of the house.
The recollection is always in my mind, and I see the picture of the Hanukkah candles on the table, the candelabra, that my father made it himself.
And the home is always in front of my eyes, and I remember those moments when we were loaded up in trucks, my mother holding the baby.
Nobody knew what the future will hold for us at that time.
As I said, the next morning we were loaded into trains.
We didn't know where our destination is.
We were loaded into trains, and then after a few days, we wound up in Izbica.
We never knew the town.
I never heard of it as a young girl.
This is in-- near Lublin.
We wound up in Izbica.
When we wound up-- when they took us out from the trains, the Judenrate from Izbica met us there.
And we were--
In the train?
Yes.
Well those are not sitting compartments.
It was trains that they loaded animals.
They transported animals-- with straw on the floor, with no way to go to a bathroom.
We had no food or drinks.
But somehow, we survived.
I guess my mother took with her certain-- certain things for-- for the children to be able to get through it.
And when we wound up in Izbica, we met-- we were met by the Judenrate, and they resettled us.
From Izbica, we went to Krasnystaw.
In Izbica, we were a few hours.
We were given water and certain food and bread.
And we were loaded up into horse wagons, family-wise.
We were loaded up two or three families into a wagon, and we were taken to Izbica-- to Krasnystaw.
That was another town a few kilometers from Izbica.
In Krasnystaw also the Judenrate decided that they had previous transports of Jews, and they don't have place for us.
And that was already winter time, and we were loaded up on-- again, on wagons, and we were take to Gorz-- Gorzkow.
I remember the snow was so high, and it was so freezing.
And the-- the horses couldn't carry in the snow-- they couldn't carry everybody.
And the man who was leading the horses advised us that adults have to walk behind the wagon.
And my father and I and my brother and-- and a lot of different adults that were with us, we walked in the snow.
And we arrived in Gorzkow early in the morning.
And over there, when we arrived, also the Judenrate resettled us to a certain Jewish families, that they had to accept us.
But the Jewish families unfortunately didn't have enough place for the whole family, so we were separated.
I was given to a different family, and my brother was given to a different family.
My mother and father and the two younger brother were also to a different Jewish family.
Than the Judenrate found for us an empty room in a different part of town, and we were located together.
You couldn't because the whole cities were surrounded by Germans, and you were scared.
But the point is that we couldn't imagine that something so tragic is going to happen to us.
We thought that-- assumed we're going to stay together.
As a family, we will stay together.
We couldn't imagine that such a tragedy is going to befell over us.
From Gorzkow--
A few months.
From Gorzjow, the Jewish committee-- the Judenrate-- relocated us to Krupe.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [SIGHS] In Krupe, I was a long time.
In Krupe, it was Zwangsarbeit, forced labor.
All the adults had to participate in Zwangsarbeit.
We were given work in two fields that belonged to a-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] in Polish, it's the dziedzic who owned all that land.
He was the owner, and he-- he could not be owner anymore because at the time, was owned from the German.
And we Jews had to go to work in the fields, Zwangsarbeit.
